reason: Both answers correctly diagnose the Goodhart's-law dynamic (bonus attracts low-intent/fraud signups), note activated users are likely flat despite doubled signups, and converge on the same core fixes: pay on activation, split the reward between referrer/referee, add fraud gates, and measure cost/incremental activated users rather than signups. Each has one small unique addition of similar value: A explicitly flags that if organic activation also dropped, the referral program isn't the cause (useful diagnostic branch to rule out confounders), while B adds a concrete fraud mitigation (delaying payout 7-14 days) and states the expected post-fix outcome so the asker knows what success looks like. These are comparable in marginal usefulness, and both are similar in length, correctness, and overall actionability with no factual errors in either.
